segments.s630-g81: وگرنه is a conditional/contrastive particle meaning “if not” or “otherwise.” ز is the poetic preposition “from/among.” مردان جنگاوران is a nominal phrase in which جنگاوران (warriors) qualifies/echoes مردان (men) — together meaning “the warlike men” or “men, the warriors”; word order follows classical/poetic Persian with the preposition before the noun phrase.
segments.s630-g82: کسی کو here functions as an interrogative/relative unit meaning “is there anyone who…”; گراید is an older/polite present/subjunctive form meaning “turns/leans/sets (one’sself)” — in context it carries the idea “would take up” or “would set himself to” (i.e. engage). به + noun marks direction/target or instrument; به گرز گران therefore indicates acting with or toward “the heavy mace.”
segments.s630-g83: گزین کن is an imperative: گزین (choose) + کن (do — imperative auxiliary), so “choose!” followed by the source phrase ز هندوستان (from India). صد سوار is the object ‘a hundred horsemen’; in poetic order the numeral and noun appear after the place phrase but together form the direct object of the imperative.
segments.s630-g84: که introduces a relative clause “who/that.” با marks opposition/encounter (“with”). یک تن از ما literally “one person of us” = “one of us” (تن here = person). کند is the present/simple verb “does/makes” used here for “engages in” or “wages”; کارزار is the object ‘battle’/‘combat.’ The whole clause means “who will do battle with one of us.”
segments.s630-g85: نخواهیم is the negative present/future first‑person plural (“we will not / we do not wish to”); the subject pronoun ما is explicit and follows the verb for emphasis (poetic inversion). باژ is the direct object “cry/clamour.” از مرز تو is a prepositional phrase “from your border/frontier” specifying source/location.
segments.s630-g86: چو is a temporal conjunction “when” (also “as/if”), and پیدا شدی is a verbal phrase “you became apparent / you proved yourself.” مردی functions as a predicate noun “a man” (i.e. you have shown manliness), and ارز تو (“your worth”) is a second conjoined predicate — the verb is elided after the first clause, so together this reads “when you became a man and (when) your worth appeared.”
segments.s630-g65: که is the subordinating/relative particle introducing this clause ('that' / 'where'). The following prepositional phrase در بیشه شیران به هنگام جنگ uses the normal Persian order: در + place (بیشه شیران, 'the lions' thicket') followed by the temporal phrase به هنگام جنگ ('at the time of battle'), with به هنگام functioning as a fixed collocation meaning 'at the time of'.
segments.s630-g66: This clause describes an action: ز آورد ایشان بخاید دو چنگ. ز + آورد is the poetic 'from/away brought' construction ('brought from' / 'came from'). ایشان is the logical source/subject 'them/their'. بخاید is an older/poetic verb form here meaning 'gapes / opens' (applied to claws/jaws), and دو چنگ is the direct object 'two claws'. Word order places the source and verb before the descriptive verb and the object, typical in poetic diction.
segments.s630-g67: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. آیین بدی is a noun (آیین) modified by the adjective بدی ('evil/bad ritual/custom'). هیچ آزاده را is the object phrase 'any / no nobleman' with را marking definiteness/emphasis; in this conditional context it functions as 'any nobleman' who might be the object of the bad custom.
segments.s630-g68: که begins a relative/subordinate clause modifying something in the prior clause. کشتی here is 'the ship', فرستاده is a past participle 'sent' modifying کشتی, and به تندی is an adverbial phrase 'with haste' (به + تندی). The final را marks the clause's definite object/topic; the whole clause reads 'the ship that was sent off in haste'.
segments.s630-g69: سرت را is the definite direct object 'your head' (را marks it). جدا is an adverb/adjective 'apart, separated' placed before the verb, and کردمی is the older/poetic past tense 'I did' ('I have done / I cut'). از تنت is the prepositional complement 'from your body' (از + تن). The phrase thus translates literally 'I have made your head separate from your body.'
segments.s630-g70: شدی is the past form 'you became'. مویه‌گر is a predicative noun 'a mourner/wailer'. The prepositional phrase بر تو پیراهنت ('over your shirt' / 'over you, your shirt') follows and indicates the object of mourning — in Persian elegiac idiom one can 'mourn over one's garment' as metonymy for lamenting. The clause therefore reads 'you became a mourner over your shirt.'
segments.s630-g71: بدو is the dative 'to him' (to that person). گفت is the verb 'said' (poetic past). بهرام is the subject 'Bahram'. کای نامدار is a vocative: کای = 'O (title/king)' and نامدار = 'renowned/famed', so the whole opening is 'Bahram said to him, “O famed Kay...”.'
segments.s630-g72: This is a conditional admonition: اگر 'if', followed by مهتری '(as) a chief/leader' (nominal), کام 'desire/wish', کژی 'crookedness/wrongness' modifying کام, and مخار an imperative/particle here used to forbid or warn (contextually 'do not take/seek'). The clause thus means 'If you are a chief, do not take crooked desire' — i.e., don't indulge in perverse or unjust wishes.
segments.s630-g1: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause ('when'); 'بشنید' is the past verb 'he heard'; 'شد' here is the copular/auxiliary 'became' that combines with the following predicate; 'نامه را' is the definite object ('the letter') marked byِ -را and placed before the predicate; 'خواستار' is a predicative adjective/participial adjective meaning 'eager/desirous' governed by 'شد' (i.e. 'became eager for the letter').
segments.s630-g2: 'شگفتی' is the noun 'astonishment/wonder'; 'بماند' is the verb 'remained/lingered' (past/imperfect sense here); 'اندران' is a locative prepositional compound meaning 'among them/among those'; 'نامدار' qualifies the group ('the renowned'). The word order is poetically inverted: literal 'astonishment remained among (the) renowned'.
segments.s630-g3: 'چو' = 'when'; 'آن نامه' = 'that letter'; 'برخواند' is the verb 'read aloud / recited' (here event of reading); word order places the verb before its subject in classical/poetic Persian; 'مرد دبیر' ('the man, the scribe') is the agent who reads (understood as subject).
segments.s630-g4: 'رخ' = 'face/visage' (here 'his face'); 'تاجور' = 'merchant/trader' (used as a type of look); 'گشت' = 'became/turned' (past of گشتن, here intransitive 'took on/turned into'); 'همچون' = 'like/as'; 'زریر' is a proper name—so the phrase means his face became like that of the merchant Zarrir. The possessor ('his') is implied from the previous clause (the scribe).
segments.s630-g5: 'بدو گفت' is a two-word clause meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'; گفت = 'said'); 'کای' is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone; 'مرد چیره‌سخن' is the addressee: 'man skilled in speech / eloquent man.' Word order is direct address following the reporting verb.
segments.s630-g6: 'به گفتار' = 'in (your) speech' (به with گفتار indicating matter/medium); 'مشتاب' is an imperative-form admonition 'do not hurry' (positive sense 'hurry!' is listed but here negated by the following مکن); 'و' = 'and'; 'تندی' = 'harshness/fierceness' (here 'harshness in tone'); 'مکن' is the prohibitive 'do not' that negates the preceding/attached verbs—together the clause is 'do not hurry in your speech, and do not be harsh.'
segments.s630-g7: 'بزرگی نماید' is a jussive/optative phrasing 'show/display greatness' (نماید here as an evaluative/presentive verb); 'همی' is a classical continuous/emphatic particle often glossed 'indeed' or 'always' added for poetic tone; 'شاه تو' is direct address 'O king / you king' (تو is the vocative/pronoun). The clause urges the king to display magnanimity.
segments.s630-g8: 'چنان هم' = 'so likewise / in the same way'; 'نماید' repeats the verb 'show/make manifest' in the same jussive/polite register; the second 'همی' is another emphatic/continuous particle; 'راه تو' = 'your way/conduct' (راه here means 'way/manner'). The whole line counsels the king to have his behavior be similarly elevated.
segments.s630-g49: A sequence of prepositional locatives listing regions: به + place (here repeated before each place) marks motion/taking place 'to/into' or, in poetic register, 'in/throughout' those lands. پاسبان is the subject 'watchmen/guards' following the locative list; classical Persian often places such adverbial/location phrases before the subject.
segments.s630-g50: نرانند is a 3rd-person plural verbal form (from راندن) placed after the subject; in this context it is used poetically/metaphorically as 'set in motion/utter' (they give voice to). جز introduces the only exception ('except'), and the noun phrase نام من بر زبان ('my name upon the tongue[s]') is the exceptional content — را is omitted but understood; word order is exception-marker + noun phrase after the verb in this clause.
segments.s630-g51: همه is the subject 'all/everyone' placed before the object and verb for emphasis. تاج ما را is a definite object marked by را ('our crown' as the thing being praised) placed before the verb (object-fronting is common in Persian with را). ستاینده‌اند is a present 3rd-person-plural verbal form built on the participial stem ستاینده + اند, meaning 'they praise' or 'they are praising'.
segments.s630-g52: پرستندگی را is the object 'worship/devotion' marked with را and fronted for emphasis; فزاینده‌اند is a 3rd-person-plural present (from فزودن) meaning 'they increase/augment'. The clause thus states that people are increasing devotion (to the crown). Word order is object (with را) + verb.
segments.s630-g53: به مشکوی here uses به in a instrumental/adverbial sense ('with/bringing a musk-like [one]') — in poetry this preposition can render a quality; مشکوی is an adjectival/epithet 'musk‑scented'. من + دخت is an older order for 'my daughter' (the possessor follows in classical word order). فغفور چین is the title + place 'the faghfur (emperor) of China' that will be the agent in the next clause.
segments.s630-g54: مرا is the accusative pronoun 'me' (written here split as م + را in the word list); خواند is the 3rd-person-singular past tense 'he called/summoned'. اندر is a locative/prepositional particle 'in/within' and جهان‌آفرین is a title 'the Creator' — together 'in the presence of the Creator' or 'before the Creator'. The clause thus reports that the Chinese faghfur summoned the speaker regarding the musk‑scented daughter, in the Creator's presence.
segments.s630-g55: پسر دارم is the straightforward Persian possession construction 'I have a son' (پسر = 'son', دارم = 'I have'). از وی indicates origin/relation 'by/from her' (i.e., born of her), and یکی شیردل is a noun phrase 'a certain one, lion‑hearted' where یکی ('a certain' / 'one') qualifies شیردل 'brave/lion‑hearted [man]'.
segments.s630-g56: The relative clause begins with که 'who/that'. بستاند is a 3rd-person singular verbal form (here used as 'takes/seizes' in sense 'wins' or 'carves out' metaphorically); از introduces the source/object 'from whom', and the second که is the subordinating/relative particle. به شمشیر دل is an instrumental phrase 'by/with the sword [of] the heart' → idiomatically 'by the sword, the heart' i.e. 'who, with his sword, can win another's heart.' The clause is a relative describing the son.
segments.s630-g73: This clause reports speech with inverted word order: 'مرا' (to me) appears up front as the indirect object, followed by the subject 'شاهِ من' (my king) and the verb 'گفت' (said). The short clause ends with the interrogative/indirect-object phrase 'کو را بگوی' (literally, 'whom/where to tell'), where 'را' marks the object and 'بگوی' is the present/imperative infinitive-like form of 'گفتن' used to mean 'tell' or 'say' in reported speech.
segments.s630-g74: 'که‌گر' is a fused conditional particle (که + گر) meaning 'if'; it introduces the admonition. 'بخردی' here is a predicative form meaning 'you are/should be wise' (classical/poetic second-person sense). 'راه' is the metaphorical 'way' and 'کژی' is 'crookedness' (the wrong course). 'مجوی' is an exhortative negative (second-person imperative/optative): 'do not seek/pursue.' Together the clause gives a conditional injunction: 'If you are wise, do not seek the crooked way.'
segments.s630-g75: 'ز درگه' is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from the court' (ز = از). 'دو دانا' is the object 'two wise men.' 'پدیدار کن' is an imperative compound: 'پدیدار' (make manifest/appear) + 'کن' (do!) — here meaning 'bring forth' or 'produce.' The whole is a command to summon two learned men from the royal court.
segments.s630-g76: 'زبان‌آور و کامران' lists the two persons/roles to be produced: 'زبان‌آور' (an eloquent speaker or spokesman) and 'کامران' (a personal name here). 'بر' + 'سخن' indicates purpose or direction toward speaking: 'to/for speech' — i.e. 'to speak' or 'for speaking.'
segments.s630-g77: The conditional particle 'گر' = 'if' opens the clause; 'ایدونک' is a small emphatic/temporal particle ('then' / 'in this case') that colors the condition. 'زیشان' = 'from them'/'of them.' 'به رای و خرد' uses 'به' in an instrumental/causal sense ('by/with') and the coordinated nouns 'رای و خرد' mean 'counsel and wisdom.' The phrase sets up the condition 'if one among them, by counsel and wisdom...'.
segments.s630-g78: 'یکی بر یکی' here presents the idea of one over another / one against another; the first 'یکی' refers to 'one (of them)' and 'بر' = 'over/above.' 'زان ما' = 'from us' (زان = from that/of that), and the verb 'بگذرد' is used in the sense 'to surpass/exceed/pass by.' The line says that if one of them in wisdom outdoes the other, he may surpass us (i.e. be allowed to pass ahead).
segments.s630-g79: This clause states personal disengagement. 'مرا' (here split orthographically) functions as the experiencer/object 'to me' and begins the clause; 'نیز' = 'also.' 'با مرزِ تو' = 'with your border/limit' (با = with; مرز = border, limit). 'کار نیست' is the frozen existential idiom 'there is no business/concern' = 'has no concern.' The whole means 'I too have nothing to do with your frontier/limit.'
segments.s630-g80: The clause beginning with 'که' gives the reason ('because/for'). 'نزدیک بخرد' is a poetic collocation meaning 'one who is close to/near the wise' (نزدیک = near; بخرد = 'wise' used adjectivally). 'سخن‌خوار' is a compound in which 'خوار' means 'despising/insulting' applied to 'سخن' — here 'one who scorns or despises speech' — and 'نیست' negates it. So the line says: 'for someone near the wise is not one who scorns speech.'
segments.s630-g41: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. هشتاد is the numeral 'eighty' modifying شاهند, which is third-person plural present 'they are (kings)'; با تاج زر is a prepositional phrase 'with a golden crown' (با = 'with', تاج = 'crown', زر = 'gold') that qualifies the kings. Word order is inverted/condensed in poetic style but follows noun + predicate pattern.
segments.s630-g42: به فرمان من is an instrumental/agent phrase 'at/by my command' (به = 'by/at', فرمان = 'command', من = 'my/I'). تنگ بسته is a compound participial/adjectival phrase 'tightly bound/tied' (تنگ 'close/tight' modifying بسته 'bound/tied'), and کمر ('waist' or 'girdle') is the object that is bound; the whole phrase is a predicate describing what the kings have done at the speaker's command.
segments.s630-g43: همه بوم ('the whole land/realm') is fronted and marked by را for definiteness/emphasis; گرد دریاست is a compressed predicate 'is round/by sea' (گرد 'around' + دریاست 'is sea') describing that the land is surrounded by sea. راه ('road/way') is left at the end to link to the following negative clause.
segments.s630-g44: نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive form 'does not come/there is no ...' or 'no ... reaches'. بدین here functions as a prepositional unit 'to this' (poetic spelling/compaction of به + این). خاک‌بر is an adjective 'dust-/earth-covered', دیو 'demon', and گاه (in the compound دیوگاه) means 'place/abode' — together 'this earth‑covered demon's abode.' The clause says that no road reaches that place.
segments.s630-g45: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; قنوج is the place-name Kannauj. تا introduces the limit 'as far as' and governs مرز دریای چین ('the border of the China Sea') — دریا('sea') with the genitive چین ('China'). The phrase lists a geographic span.
segments.s630-g46: Again ز 'from' begins a second span; سقلاب is a place-name here (obscure or archaic). تا 'as far as' combines with پیش 'before/upon/in front of' to indicate the endpoint 'before the land of Iran' (ایران‌زمین). The whole segment names the other extent of the territory.
segments.s630-g47: بزرگان 'the great ones/personages' is the plural subject; همه 'all' emphasizes totality. زیردست is a predicative/adjectival noun 'underling/under my control', and منند is an archaic/copulative form meaning 'are like' or functioning here as the copula 'are' — so the clause asserts that all great men are subject to the speaker.
segments.s630-g48: به بیچارگی is an adverbial phrase 'through/by misery' (به = 'by/through', بیچارگی = 'misery/helplessness'). در + پرست (در 'in', پرست 'worshipper/worship') forms 'in worship' or 'as worshippers', and منند again functions as a copular/qualifying word 'they are' or 'they act as'. The clause says that, in their helplessness, they act as worshippers of the speaker.
segments.s630-g9: کسی is an indefinite subject here ('anyone'). باژ is a noun 'cry/shout' placed before the verb in poetic order. خواهد is the 3rd-person singular present/future auxiliary 'will/would' (here rendering a hypothetical/conditional sense). ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and هندوستان is the place 'India'. Together they form a conditional clause 'if anyone will cry out from India...'.
segments.s630-g10: نباشم is the 1st-person negative present/subjunctive form of 'to be' meaning 'I am not / I will not be' and denies membership in the following group. ز is 'of/from' introducing the source/relationship. گوینده is 'the speaker/narrator' and همداستان is an adjective/noun meaning 'like‑minded / in agreement'. The phrase means 'I am not one who agrees with the speaker.'
segments.s630-g11: به لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'to the army' (به + لشکر). همی‌گوید is the habitual/present 'he says / he kept saying' (repetitive aspect). این‌گر is a conditioned/contrastive particle 'if this is so / but if' that launches the conditional clause, and به گنج means 'about/for treasure' (به + گنج). The clause functions like 'he kept telling the army: if this is about treasure...'.
segments.s630-g12: وگر begins a second conditional 'or if'. شهر و کشور are the coordinated direct objects 'city and country/realm' placed before the infinitive. سپردن is the infinitive 'to hand over / to entrust', and به رنج ('to hardship') states the consequence/cost. The whole segment reads 'or if it is to hand over city and country to hardship.' Note the object-before-infinitive word order typical of Persian.
segments.s630-g13: کلنگ‌اند is a predicative phrase meaning roughly 'are pickaxes' (poetic/metaphoric copula with plural -اند). شاهان is the plural subject 'kings'. و من 'and I' introduces contrast; چون is the simile marker 'like/as', and عقاب is 'eagle'. The clause sets up a contrast between what kings are like and what the speaker is like.
segments.s630-g14: وگر is conditional/contrastive 'but if'. خاک 'earth/dust' is presented as an alternative state for the (implied) others. و من 'and I' again contrasts the speaker, همچو is another simile word 'such as / like', دریای is the ezāfe form of دریا 'sea', and آب 'water' completes the image 'like the sea of water'. The clause means 'but if (they are) dust, I am like the sea.'
segments.s630-g15: کسی here with the negative verb yields a universal negation 'no one'. با ستاره 'with a star' (instrumental/against the star) is the prepositional phrase. نکوشد is the negative present/subjunctive of کوشیدن 'to strive/attempt' (here 'does not attempt'); به جنگ 'in battle' marks the domain. The clause means 'no one attempts to do battle with the stars.'
segments.s630-g16: نه 'nor' links a second negative clause. با آسمان 'with heaven/sky' is the complement. جست is a verbal form of جستن here, 'sought/contended (with)' or 'strove against' (poetic usage); the clause is elliptical and uses کس ('anyone') after the verb as the logical subject/object. نام و ننگ is the collocation 'name and shame' meaning 'fame and disgrace'. The line says in effect 'nor does anyone contend with heaven for fame and disgrace.'
segments.s630-g33: همان is a demonstrative ('that' / 'that very') modifying the following noun phrase. کوه و دریای گوهر is a coordinated noun phrase ('mountain and sea of gems') where دریای گوهر is a genitive/construct: 'sea of jewels.' مراست is an archaic contraction of مرا + است ('mara‑ast' = 'is to me' / 'belongs to me'), i.e. a predicative 'is mine.'
segments.s630-g34: This line shows classical Persian word order and inversion: به من (to/for me) is the indirect object, دارد is the main verb 'has/holds/gives', اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now', جهان is the subject 'the world' and پشت راست is the object/complement meaning 'a straight/firm back' (i.e. support). Literally: 'To me has now the world a firm back.'
segments.s630-g35: همان again points back as 'that/the same' and modifies چشمه ('spring, source'). عنبر و عود و مشک are three coordinated nouns modifying or enumerating what issues from that spring (ambergris, agarwood/oud, and musk). The coordinator و links them in a normal list.
segments.s630-g36: دگر here means 'another/other.' گنج کافور is a noun phrase 'treasure (of) camphor' with کافور specifying the kind of treasure. ناگشته is a prefixed negative/past participial form meaning 'not become / not yet formed,' which modifies خشک ('dry'), so the phrase says the camphor-treasure is 'not yet dried.'
segments.s630-g37: دگر 'another' modifies داروی ('remedy, medicine'). مردم دردمند is a genitive chain where مردم ('the people') is specified by دردمند ('suffering/afflicted'), so the whole phrase means 'another remedy for the afflicted people.' Word order is noun + modifiers/attributes following it.
segments.s630-g38: به روی زمین is a locative phrase 'on/upon the face of the earth' (به + روی + زمین). هرک is an indefinite 'whoever/whosoever.' گردد is the classical present/subjunctive/third‑person 'becomes / happens to be.' نژند is a predicative adjective meaning 'base / despicable.' The clause therefore reads 'whoever on earth becomes base.'
segments.s630-g39: همه بوم ('the whole homeland') is the direct object; ما را uses ما + را so that 'ما' is marked as the possessor/affected party ('from us' / 'our ...'). بدین‌سان is an adverb 'in this manner/thus.' برست is an archaic past form (third person) meaning 'took away / carried off / was carried off' (here 'it took away our whole land'). The sentence follows older Persian order where the verb comes after the adverb and object.
segments.s630-g40: اگر ... است sets up a conditional/hypothetical 'if there is / if there was'; زر و سیم ('gold and silver') are coordinated nouns as the first case, و‌گر is the correlative 'or if' introducing the alternative, and گوهر‌ست is an archaic fused form 'is jewel' (گوهر + است) meaning 'or if it is jewels.' Together: 'whether it was gold and silver, or jewels.'
segments.s630-g17: This is a comparative clause built with 'X بهتر از Y' = 'X is better than Y'. 'هنر' is the topic (here: virtue/skill), followed by 'بهتر از' and a gerund phrase 'گفتن نابکار' ('saying/calling [someone] wicked') functioning as the thing being compared. 'گفتن' is a masdar/infinitive meaning 'saying/calling'.
segments.s630-g18: 'که' introduces an explanatory/relative clause. 'گیرد' (3sg of گرفتن) here means 'makes/takes' (causes); word order is inverted for poetic effect (verb then object 'ترا' then subject phrase 'مرد داننده' and predicate 'خوار'). 'ت' is the 2nd-person enclitic object, and 'را' is the definite/object marker/emphatic; 'مرد داننده خوار' means 'a knowing/man who knows will deem (you) contemptible'.
segments.s630-g19: A negative coordination using repeated 'نه ... نه' to list missing things: 'neither X nor Y ...'. The nouns are given in a row (mardī, dānesh, keshvar, shahr) and function as coordinated objects/subjects in the negative statement.
segments.s630-g20: 'ز' (classical/poetic) gives origin/reason ('from/because of'); 'شاهی شما' = 'your kingship' with the possessor placed after the noun (poetic order). The particle 'را' here marks/ emphasizes the definite element. 'زبانست' is a fused predicate ('زبان است') meaning 'is (mere) speech/talk' and 'بهر' at the end means 'for/'or 'for the sake of' — overall: 'for your kingship there is only speech' (i.e. your kingship is mere words).
segments.s630-g21: 'نهفته' is a past-participial adjective 'hidden'. The phrase 'همه بوم' (= 'throughout the land/home') modifies 'گنج'. 'گنج منست' is a compressed/poetic clause: noun + (copula + possessor); here it conveys 'the treasure is (mine / in my keeping)'. (Note: the printed senses for 'منست' do not contain the obvious 'is mine' meaning; in the line it functions as the copula + possessor.)
segments.s630-g22: Subject 'نیاکان' (ancestors) + indirect object 'بدو' ('to it', i.e. to that treasure). 'هیچ' here = 'never / not at all' (negative adverb), 'نابرده' is a negative participle 'untaken/never touched', and 'دست' (= 'hand') completes the idiom meaning 'never laid a hand on it'. Word order is compact and typical of classical poetry.
segments.s630-g23: 'دگر' = 'another' or 'else/again' placed before the noun. The phrase 'گنج برگستوان و زره' groups 'treasure' with two loci/means (arm/strength and armor). The exact lexical role of 'برگستوان' is obscure/poetic here (likely 'in the brawn/arm' or similar); 'و زره' simply adds 'and armor'.
segments.s630-g24: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause here ('when'). 'گنجور ما' = 'our treasury/treasure-house' (possessor after noun, poetic order). 'برگشاید' is the present/subjunctive form of 'بازگشودن/گشودن' meaning 'opens' or 'discloses' in subordinate clause use. 'گره' = 'knot/lock/problem' — so the line reads 'when our treasury opens the knot/lock'.
segments.s630-g25: به پیلانش باید کشیدن کلید functions as a clause meaning 'to make his elephants move one must pull the key.' به + NP (پیلانش) here marks the domain/target ('with/regarding his elephants'); باید is the modal 'must/it is necessary'; کشیدن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to pull' serving as the complement of باید; کلید is the direct object of کشیدن ('the key' to be pulled). Word order is topical (target first) then necessity + infinitive + object.
segments.s630-g26: وگر ژنده پیلش تواند کشید is a conditional follow-up: وگر 'but/if' introduces the alternative; ژنده modifies پیلش = 'his ragged elephant'; تواند (تواند) is the modal 'is able/can' (present tense, third person); کشید here functions as the predicate 'be pulled' (passive/result sense with توانستن). The clause therefore says 'but if his elephant is ragged, it can be pulled.'
segments.s630-g27: وگر introduces a hypothetical 'if/but if.' گیری is the verbal noun/presentive 'taking/you take' (here the act of taking up arms) and is governed by وگر; از marks the things taken ('from' or 'up' in the sense of 'take up') and links تیغ و جوشن ('sword and cuirass/armor') as the objects; شمار ('number/ranks') is the noun that will become the subject of the following verb (it is topicalized here).
segments.s630-g28: ستاره is the subject 'the stars' or 'the star‑ranks'; شود is the verbal copula 'become' (future/resultative); پیش چشم تو is a prepositional phrase 'before your eyes' with پیش 'before/in front of' + possessive pronoun تو; خوار is the predicative adjective 'despised/humbled.' Together: 'the stars will become humbled before your eyes.'
segments.s630-g29: زمین بر نتابد سپاه مرا is a clause where زمین 'the earth' is the subject and بر نتابد (negated نتابد, from تابیدن in the sense 'to bear/endure') is the predicate 'does not/cannot bear.' سپاه مرا is the object 'my host/army' (مرا here functions as a first‑person marker attached to سپاه in the poem). The verbal negation and the verb in present/future sense express that the earth cannot sustain his army.
segments.s630-g30: همان ژنده پیلان و گاه مرا is a nominal apposition specifying that army: همان 'that/same'; ژنده modifies پیلان (پیلان = 'elephants') giving 'the same ragged elephants'; و گاه مرا adds 'and my camp/time' (گاه functioning as 'camp/occasion/time' here as part of the tableau). Together the phrase further describes the host the earth cannot bear.
segments.s630-g31: هزار ار به هندی زنی در هزار is a hypothetical framing: هزار 'a thousand' + ار (classical conditional 'if') introduces the supposition. به هندی 'in/of India' qualifies the following noun; زنی is the noun 'woman' (here part of the hypothetical population unit), and در هزار ('in a thousand') repeats/counts the thousand‑unit idea — the clause sketches an imagined multitude (a thousand Indian women per thousand). The clause is elliptical/poetic and sets up the conditional for the next clause.
segments.s630-g32: بود کس که خواند مرا شهریار is the apodosis: بود (past/copular 'would be/was') introduces existence in the hypothetical; کس 'someone/anyone' is the indefinite subject; که خواند is a relative clause with خواند 'to call/summon' (here 'would call'); مرا is the object pronoun 'me'; شهریار is the predicative noun 'king.' The whole means 'there would be someone who would call me king.'
segments.s630-g57: ز هنگام ... تا is a temporal prepositional phrase: ز (poetic 'from') + هنگام ('at the time of') names the starting point, and تا ('until') introduces the end-point (کیقباد). Proper names follow directly; the whole segment means 'from the time of Kay Kavus until Kay Qobad.'
segments.s630-g58: ازین بوم و بر is a locative phrase 'from this bôm and bar (land and region)' where از = 'from'; این is split orthographically as 'ین' here. کس نکرده‌ست یاد is a negated existential/performative clause: کس ('anyone/no one') is the indefinite subject, and یاد + نکرده‌ست literally 'has not done remembrance' = 'has not been remembered/mentioned.'
segments.s630-g59: همان نامبردار is a pointing/identifying phrase: همان = 'that/the very', نامبردار = 'aforementioned/renowned'; the numeral phrase (سیصد هزار) follows as apposition ('three hundred thousand').
segments.s630-g60: ز لشکر ('of/from the army') specifies the group. The relative clause introduced by که follows: خواند is past tense 'called/summoned' (here sense 'to call/summon'), and Persian poetic word order places the agent شهریار ('the king') after the verb and the object (مرا + را) before it; 'مرا' is split orthographically (here represented as 'م' + 'را').
segments.s630-g61: ز پیوستگانم ... is an enumerative phrase: ز = 'of/from', پیوستگانم = 'my followers/attendants' (possessive -م attached). The numeral (هزار و دویست) follows the noun as a count apposition in Persian.
segments.s630-g62: کزیشان = 'from/of them' (کز = 'from which/of which'); the clause کسی را ... نیست is a negative existential: کسی را (object-marked 'anyone') ... نیست = 'there is no one.' به من راه ('a way/path to me') is the phrase negated by نیست = 'there is no way to me' or 'no one has access to me.'
segments.s630-g63: همه زاد بر زاد خویش منند is a nominal + copula sentence: همه = 'all/everyone', زاد (birth/origin) repeated in the idiom زاد بر زاد خویش = 'of/from their own birth/stock', and منند is the adjectival copula 'are like/are such.' The phrase states a likeness/shared origin.
segments.s630-g64: که introduces a relative clause: در هند = 'in India', بر پای پیش = 'on foot before/in the presence of' (بر + پای = 'on foot', پیش = 'in front of/before'), and منند repeats the copular 'are like/stand as.' The clause qualifies the preceding noun phrase.

sentences.s630-s41: If not, is there anyone among the warlike men who would take up the heavy mace?
sentences.s630-s42: Choose a hundred horsemen from India who will do battle with one of us.
sentences.s630-s43: We will not raise a cry from your border, now that you have shown yourself a man and your worth.
sentences.s630-s33: That in the lions' thicket, at the time of battle, two claws gaped forth from them.
sentences.s630-s34: If you perform an evil rite against any nobleman — the ship that was sent off in haste —
sentences.s630-s35: I have cut off your head from your body; you have become a mourner over your shirt.
sentences.s630-s36: Bahram said to him, 'O famed Kay, if you (claim to) be a leader, do not take crooked desire.'
sentences.s630-s1: When he heard the letter, he became eager for it; astonishment lingered among the renowned.
sentences.s630-s2: When that letter was read aloud, the scribe's face took on the look of the merchant Zarrir.
sentences.s630-s3: He said to him, 'O eloquent man, do not hurry in your speech, and do not be harsh.'
sentences.s630-s4: Show greatness, O king; likewise let your conduct be so.
sentences.s630-s25: In India, in China, and in Khotan the watchmen utter nothing on their tongues except my name.
sentences.s630-s26: All praise our crown; they increase the devotion paid to (it).
sentences.s630-s27: The faghfur (emperor) of China summoned me for my musk‑scented daughter in the presence of the Creator.
sentences.s630-s28: I have by her a son, a lion‑hearted one, who can, with his sword, win the heart of anyone.
sentences.s630-s37: My king said to me, "Whom shall I tell? — If you are wise, do not pursue the crooked way."
sentences.s630-s38: From the court summon two wise men; bring forth a spokesman and Kamran to speak.
sentences.s630-s39: If, then, among them one (in) counsel and wisdom should surpass the other, let him pass beyond us.
sentences.s630-s40: I too have no business with your frontier, for one who is near the wise is not contemptuous of speech.
sentences.s630-s21: When eighty kings, wearing golden crowns, have tightly bound their waists at my command.
sentences.s630-s22: The whole realm is ringed by the sea; no road reaches this earth‑covered demon's lair.
sentences.s630-s23: From Kannauj to the border of the China Sea; from S(a)qlāb to the approaches of the land of Iran.
sentences.s630-s24: All the great ones are beneath my hand; in their misery they are my worshippers.
sentences.s630-s5: If anyone cries out from India, I will not be one who sides with the speaker.
sentences.s630-s6: He kept saying to the army: 'If this is for treasure— or if it is to hand over city and country to hardship.'
sentences.s630-s7: Kings are pickaxes, and I am like an eagle; but if (they are) dust, I am like the sea of water.
sentences.s630-s8: No one attempts to fight the stars in battle; nor does anyone contend with heaven for fame and disgrace.
sentences.s630-s17: That mountain and sea of jewels are mine; the world now gives me a firm backing.
sentences.s630-s18: That same spring of amber, oud, and musk; another treasure of camphor not yet dried.
sentences.s630-s19: Another remedy for the suffering people; whoever on the face of the earth becomes base.
sentences.s630-s20: Thus all our homeland was taken from us — whether it was gold and silver, or whether it was jewels.
sentences.s630-s9: Virtue (skill) is better than calling someone wicked; that will make a knowing man hold you in contempt.
sentences.s630-s10: There is neither manhood nor learning, neither realm nor city; for your kingship there is only talk.
sentences.s630-s11: All the land's treasure lies hidden; my ancestors never once laid a hand on it.
sentences.s630-s12: Another treasure is in the arm and in armor; when our treasury opens its knot...
sentences.s630-s13: To set his elephants in motion you must pull the key; but if his elephant is ragged, it can be pulled.
sentences.s630-s14: But if you take sword and cuirass, the very ranks of the stars will be humbled before your eyes.
sentences.s630-s15: The earth cannot bear my host — that same ragged herd of elephants and my camp.
sentences.s630-s16: If there were a thousand (such) — if a thousand Indian women (were counted by the thousand) — there would be someone who would call me king.
sentences.s630-s29: From the time of Kay Kavus to Kay Qobad, no one from this land and region has been remembered.
sentences.s630-s30: That very aforementioned three hundred thousand of the army whom the king summoned me to.
sentences.s630-s31: Among my followers there are one thousand two hundred, and of them there is no one who has access to me.
sentences.s630-s32: They are all sprung from their own stock; they are the sort who in India stand before me on foot.
